description: A 2D convolutional neural network architecture diagram showing two stacked convolutional layers applied to a 9x5 input grid, illustrating the full modular CNN pipeline
teaching_value: Provides a high-level visual overview of how multiple convolutional layers compose to form a deep CNN, motivating the modular perspective introduced in the article
when_to_show: Show at the start of a CNN introduction lesson as a motivating overview before breaking down individual components
suggested_caption: Full 2D CNN architecture with two stacked convolutional layers. (Olah, 2014)

description: A diagram of 9 evenly-spaced 1D audio sample inputs connected to a fully-connected layer, representing the naive baseline approach to audio classification
teaching_value: Establishes the baseline fully-connected approach so students can contrast it with the convolutional approach, highlighting why weight sharing is beneficial
when_to_show: Show when introducing the motivation for convolutional layers — use as the 'before' case before presenting the convolutional alternative
suggested_caption: Naive fully-connected network applied to 9 audio samples. (Olah, 2014)

description: A diagram showing a single neuron group A applied to a local window of 1D audio samples, illustrating the concept of local feature detection with a shared filter
teaching_value: Concretely shows how a single neuron group A scans a local window, making the concept of local receptive fields and translational symmetry tangible
when_to_show: Show when explaining why convolutions exploit local structure and translational symmetry in sequential data
suggested_caption: Neuron group A detecting local features in a 1D audio signal. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 1D convolutional layer diagram where neuron group A with a window of 2 points slides across 9 input samples, feeding into a fully-connected layer F
teaching_value: Shows the mechanics of a 1D convolutional layer with a small window size, illustrating how shared weights produce multiple outputs fed into a downstream layer
when_to_show: Show when first introducing the convolutional layer operation — use to explain the sliding window and weight sharing mechanism
suggested_caption: 1D convolutional layer A (window=2) feeding into fully-connected layer F. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 1D convolutional layer diagram where neuron group A uses a window of 3 points, feeding into a fully-connected layer, showing a slightly larger receptive field
teaching_value: Demonstrates how increasing the window size of a convolutional layer affects the architecture, helping students understand the receptive field parameter
when_to_show: Show after the 2-point window example to illustrate how window size is a tunable parameter in convolutional layers
suggested_caption: 1D convolutional layer A with window size 3 over 9 inputs. (Olah, 2014)

description: A diagram of two stacked 1D convolutional layers: neuron group A feeds into neuron group B, which then connects to a fully-connected layer, illustrating layer composability
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates that convolutional layers are composable — stacking them allows the network to learn hierarchical, increasingly abstract features
when_to_show: Show when teaching how deep CNNs are built by stacking convolutional layers, emphasizing the hierarchical abstraction benefit
suggested_caption: Two stacked 1D convolutional layers A and B with shared weights. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 1D CNN architecture diagram showing a convolutional layer, followed by a max-pooling layer, followed by another convolutional layer, illustrating the Conv-Pool-Conv pattern
teaching_value: Illustrates the classic Conv-Pool-Conv architectural pattern, showing how max-pooling provides spatial invariance between convolutional layers
when_to_show: Show when introducing max-pooling — use to explain why pooling is inserted between convolutional layers and what invariance it provides
suggested_caption: 1D CNN with Conv-MaxPool-Conv architecture for translation invariance. (Olah, 2014)

description: A diagram of a single 2D convolutional unit showing neuron group A looking at a small 2D patch of an input image grid, illustrating the 2D receptive field concept
teaching_value: Bridges the gap from 1D to 2D convolution by showing how the receptive field becomes a 2D patch rather than a 1D segment, critical for understanding image CNNs
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from 1D to 2D convolutions — use to make the extension to image data concrete and intuitive
suggested_caption: Single 2D convolutional unit A scanning a patch of an image. (Olah, 2014)

description: A full 2D convolutional layer diagram showing neuron group A sliding over a 9x5 input grid, producing a feature map output connected to a fully-connected layer
teaching_value: Shows the complete 2D convolutional layer operation over a 2D input, making explicit how the sliding patch produces a 2D feature map for image recognition tasks
when_to_show: Show when teaching 2D CNNs for image data — use to explain how a single convolutional layer processes an entire image via sliding patches
suggested_caption: 2D convolutional layer A applied to a 9×5 image input. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 2D CNN architecture diagram showing a 9x5 input processed by two stacked 2D convolutional layers with a 2D max-pooling layer in between
teaching_value: Presents the full 2D Conv-Pool-Conv architecture used in image recognition, allowing students to see how all components (2D conv, 2D pooling, stacking) combine
when_to_show: Show as a capstone diagram when summarizing 2D CNN architecture — use to consolidate understanding of all components working together on image data
suggested_caption: Full 2D CNN: Conv-MaxPool-Conv architecture on a 9×5 image. (Olah, 2014)

description: Diagram showing the standard convolutional layer module A as a set of parallel neurons all receiving the same input region and computing different features simultaneously
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the internal structure of the A module in a conv layer — students can see that multiple neurons process the same receptive field in parallel, each learning a different feature detector
when_to_show: When introducing the internal structure of a convolutional layer, specifically to explain what happens inside a single sliding-window application
suggested_caption: Standard conv layer: parallel neurons share input, learn distinct features. (Olah, 2014)

description: Diagram illustrating the Network-in-Network (NIN) Mlpconv variant of module A, showing multiple stacked layers of neurons inside A rather than a single parallel layer, enabling higher-level feature extraction per region
teaching_value: Contrasts the NIN architecture with standard conv layers, helping students understand how replacing a single neuron layer with a mini-MLP inside each local patch can extract more abstract features
when_to_show: After explaining standard conv layers, when discussing architectural innovations or the concept of increasing representational power within a local receptive field
suggested_caption: Network-in-Network: stacked neuron layers inside each conv module A. (Olah, 2014)

description: Architecture diagram of the AlexNet (Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton 2012) deep convolutional neural network, showing 5 convolutional layers with pooling and 3 fully-connected layers split across two GPU streams
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete reference architecture for a landmark deep CNN, illustrating depth, layer types, and the practical engineering choice of splitting computation across two GPUs
when_to_show: When teaching landmark CNN architectures or motivating why depth and scale matter; use as a case study after explaining individual layer types
suggested_caption: AlexNet architecture: 5 conv + 3 FC layers across two GPUs. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)

description: Sample classification results from AlexNet showing input images alongside the network's top predicted categories, including cases where errors are visually understandable
teaching_value: Grounds abstract accuracy numbers in concrete examples, helping students develop intuition for what 63% top-1 accuracy looks like and why some errors are reasonable given visual similarity
when_to_show: When discussing model evaluation metrics or building intuition about CNN performance; effective for motivating why top-5 accuracy is a useful metric alongside top-1
suggested_caption: AlexNet ImageNet results: correct and near-miss classifications shown. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)

description: Visualization of learned first-layer filters from AlexNet, showing one group of neurons that learned oriented edge detectors (black and white) and another group that learned color/texture detectors (color contrasts and patterns)
teaching_value: Demonstrates that CNNs learn interpretable, structured features from data without human supervision — edge detectors and color detectors emerge purely from gradient-based training on image classification
when_to_show: When explaining what CNNs actually learn, or when motivating why convolutional architectures are well-suited to visual data; powerful for dispelling the 'black box' misconception
suggested_caption: AlexNet first-layer filters: edge detectors and color patterns emerge from training. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)

description: Diagram of a 1D convolutional neural network showing input nodes x_n connected through sliding module A to output nodes y_n, illustrating the formal relationship y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) with the convolution window sliding along the sequence
teaching_value: Provides a clean formal visualization of 1D convolution, connecting the mathematical notation to a node-and-edge diagram so students can see exactly which inputs feed each output
when_to_show: When formalizing the convolution operation mathematically; show alongside the equation y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) to bridge diagram and formula
suggested_caption: 1D conv layer: output y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) via sliding window. (Olah, 2014)

description: Diagram of a 2D convolutional neural network showing a 5x5 (or similar) input grid connected through a 2D sliding module A to a 2D output grid, illustrating the formal 2D convolution relationship y_{n,m} = A(x_{n,m}, x_{n+1,m}, ...)
teaching_value: Extends the 1D convolution diagram to 2D, helping students visualize how the receptive field becomes a 2D patch and how the output grid is produced — essential for understanding image convolutions
when_to_show: Immediately after the 1D convolution diagram, when generalizing to 2D inputs like images; pair with the 2D index notation equation to reinforce the connection
suggested_caption: 2D conv layer: y_{n,m} computed from a 2D patch of inputs. (Olah, 2014)

description: Diagram of a fully-connected (regular) neural network with multiple hidden layers, showing neurons as circles connected by edges across layers, illustrating the dense connectivity pattern
teaching_value: Helps students visualize why fully-connected networks don't scale to images — every neuron connects to every input, making the parameter count explode for even small images like 32x32x3
when_to_show: Show when introducing why CNNs are needed — use this to contrast with convolutional layers and motivate local connectivity
suggested_caption: Regular fully-connected neural network; scales poorly to image inputs. (cs231n)

description: Architecture diagram of a ConvNet pipeline showing the transformation of a 3D input image volume through CONV, RELU, POOL, and FC layers, with each stage depicted as a 3D block shrinking spatially but varying in depth
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of how data flows through a CNN as a sequence of 3D volumes, distinguishing layers with learnable parameters (CONV, FC) from fixed-function layers (RELU, POOL)
when_to_show: Show at the start of CNN architecture discussion as an overview; revisit when explaining each layer type in detail
suggested_caption: ConvNet pipeline: INPUT→CONV→RELU→POOL→FC for CIFAR-10. (cs231n)

description: 3D diagram of a convolutional layer input volume with a highlighted column of neurons showing local 2D spatial connectivity (receptive field) but full depth connectivity, illustrating the asymmetric connectivity principle
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the key CNN concept that neurons connect locally in width/height but fully across depth, helping students understand why this reduces parameters compared to fully-connected layers
when_to_show: Show when explaining local connectivity in Conv layers, especially to contrast spatial locality with full depth connectivity
suggested_caption: Depth column: local 2D receptive field, full depth connectivity. (cs231n)

description: Diagram of a single neuron model showing weighted inputs being summed and passed through an activation function, representing the basic computational unit used in convolutional layers
teaching_value: Reminds students of the fundamental neuron computation (weighted sum + activation) that underlies each unit in a convolutional layer, grounding the abstract layer diagrams in concrete math
when_to_show: Show when students need a refresher on the basic neuron computation before explaining how filters in a Conv layer perform the same operation over local patches
suggested_caption: Single neuron: weighted inputs summed and passed through activation. (cs231n)

description: Diagram illustrating the effect of stride on the spatial arrangement of output neurons, showing how a filter slides across a 1D or 2D input with different stride values, producing different output sizes
teaching_value: Concretely shows students how stride controls the step size of filter movement and directly determines output spatial dimensions, making the output size formula intuitive
when_to_show: Show when teaching the three hyperparameters (depth, stride, zero-padding) that control output volume size in a Conv layer
suggested_caption: Stride controls filter step size and output spatial dimensions. (cs231n)

description: Visualization of parameter sharing in a convolutional layer, showing that all neurons in a single depth slice share the same filter weights, with different depth slices using different filters (shown as colored weight maps)
teaching_value: Makes the parameter sharing assumption tangible — students can see that one learned filter is applied everywhere spatially, dramatically reducing parameters from millions to thousands
when_to_show: Show when explaining how CNNs reduce parameter count via weight sharing; use to contrast with the 105M parameter count of a fully-connected first layer
suggested_caption: Parameter sharing: one filter per depth slice applied across all positions. (cs231n)

description: Diagram showing a pooling layer operating on a 3D input volume, illustrating how it reduces spatial dimensions (width and height) independently per depth slice while preserving depth
teaching_value: Helps students visualize that pooling acts slice-by-slice on the depth dimension and only reduces spatial size, clarifying what 'spatial downsampling' means in practice
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Pooling layer to illustrate its effect on volume dimensions before discussing the MAX operation in detail
suggested_caption: Pooling layer reduces spatial size per depth slice, preserving depth. (cs231n)

description: Illustration of max pooling operation on a 4x4 input grid divided into 2x2 regions, showing how the maximum value is selected from each region to produce a 2x2 output, with specific numbers as examples
teaching_value: Provides a concrete numerical example of max pooling, making the MAX operation and 75% activation discard rate immediately understandable with actual values
when_to_show: Show when explaining the max pooling operation in detail; the numerical example makes the abstract operation concrete and easy to verify by hand
suggested_caption: Max pooling: 2×2 filter, stride 2 selects maximum from each region. (cs231n)
